text: The surgeon begins colonic mobilization, inspecting the colon thoroughly for vascular pedicles and any abdominal wall adhesions.

The surgeon observes the splenic flexure, which is adherent to the parietal peritoneum. The existing ports from the hepatotomy are being utilized, and an additional port may be required to aid in the mobilization of the descending colon.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar for battery section, preparing the surgical field for further dissection.

The assistant grasps the distal transverse colon and pulls it upward to expose the left colic vessels at their root. The surgeon identifies the vessels, with the pancreas visibly protruding from the mesentery of the colon, marking the root of the mesocolic vessels.

The surgeon begins dissection by dividing the peritoneum above the level of the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure, revealing the root of the left side of the mesocolon. A major vessel is visible, and the peritoneum and its attachments are carefully separated layer by layer to minimize bleeding.
The surgeon continues dissection in a classical V pattern, focusing on clearing lymph nodes at the root of the mesentery, despite dealing with stage four disease.

The assistant provides continuous traction by grabbing the area near the mass, either the mesentery or the tinea of the colon. The surgeon identifies and dissects the middle colic vessel at its root, revealing a cluster of lymph nodes around the mesenteric vessels, and proceeds to dissect the vein.

The surgeon identifies multiple vessels near the root of the mesocolon, including the left colic vessel, middle colic vessel, and several veins. Dr. Neeraj holds one of the vessels while the surgeon plans to dissect and clip them after removing lymph nodes and lymphatic tissue. The growth is visible, and the surgeon determines the margin for resection.
The surgeon divides a vessel near the middle colic side, while multiple lymph nodes are visible in the area. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the lymph nodes along with the specimen.

The surgeon identifies a Y-shaped junction of vessels, deciding to preserve the middle colic vessel while dissecting and removing lymph nodes from the common origin area, amidst the stage four disease context.
The surgeon has completed nodal clearance on the specimen side and is preparing to divide a vessel, with visible nodes cleared from the right side.
The surgeon uses a tissue vac applicator to hold a vessel, likely the left colic vessel, near the pancreas, preparing to take it while considering patient comorbidities.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the pancreas and the base of the left colic vessels. The patient's lean physique exposes nearby anatomical structures without a fat cushion, making the pulsating vessels clearly visible.
The surgeon requests a gauze piece and applies traction to the tissue, preparing to use a clip for vessel or tissue management.
The surgeon has taken the pedicle along with the inferior side of the pancreas, revealing nodes and vessels in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and prepares to clip it using a Hem-o-lok clip. The assistant provides traction to expose the vessel, allowing for precise placement of the clip.

The surgeon navigates along the anterior border of the pancreas, identifying the mesentery of the colon above and the pancreas below, while adjusting the surgical approach to maintain proper orientation.

The surgeon delineates the inferior border of the pancreas, identifying the mesocolon and possibly the left colic vein draining into the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The Girota and kidney are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection and resection of the left artery and vein, applying a hemlock clip at the root. All lymph nodes have been cleared from the base of the vessels.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mesentery, which is densely adhered to the posterior wall, revealing the pancreas and kidney. The medial to lateral mobilization of the colon is nearly complete.
The surgeon begins mobilization towards the descending colon, revealing the first sigmoid branch arising from the left colic artery. The dissection exposes the DG flexure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mass along with delicate vessels, ensuring that the active surgical field remains clear of the duodenum and other vital structures during the close space dissection.
The surgeon identifies the kidney and makes a peritoneal incision on the mesentery of the colon.
The surgeon carefully handles a vessel with a grasper, applying pressure before clipping it using vascular laparoscopic instruments to preserve the vessel and maintain blood flow.

The surgeon uses the same port previously utilized for hepatectomy, creating an unusual angle for the procedure. The cleared root is visible from the inferior surface of the pancreas to the DJ flexure, with flimsy tissue and the Gerota fascia exposed.
The surgeon separates the colon, lifting the Gerota's fascia along with the mesocolon. The plane between the Gerota's fascia and the mesocolon is visible.

The surgeon points out the DJ flexure, which has been completely mobilized. The left colic vein and inferior mesenteric vein are visible passing behind the structures. Renal vessels are identified, along with a thin gonadal vessel. Pedicles near the growth have been taken, and the growth is visible in the lower part of the field, indicating the area for further dissection.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the lateral side of the colon, carefully dividing it from its parietal adhesions. The procedure involves mobilizing both the descending and sigmoid colon as part of the left colectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the distal part of the colon, separating the descending colon from surrounding adhesions in the correct anatomical plane to facilitate a healthy anastomosis.
The surgeon encounters flimsy adhesions in the surgical field and considers using a monopolar diathermy for dissection.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure near the growth area, carefully avoiding direct traction over the site of the growth during dissection to minimize potential tumor cell seeding.

The surgeon approaches the lateral side of the colon, having already addressed the pedicles near the growth. Adhesions are visible, which sometimes act as natural retractors during the dissection process.

The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure, noting its higher position compared to the hepatic flexure. Dense adhesions and lateral vascular attachments of the omentum and parietal peritoneum are visible, contrasting with the easier mobilization of the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon encounters a thin line of flimsy areolar tissue, with the Girota fascia appearing so thin that it is difficult to visually distinguish.
The surgeon secures a distal margin of the colon, ensuring at least fifteen centimeters in length, before dividing the mesentery of the colon.

The surgeon progresses the dissection towards the splenic flexure within the mesentery, utilizing either a medial or lateral approach. The patient's thin physique allows flexibility in choosing the dissection side.
The surgeon exposes the splenic flexure, with the pancreas visible below it.

The surgeon clips a vessel associated with the transverse colon, indicating it will be included in the specimen. The future line of demarcation is marked by an arch vessel located just below the harmonic scalpel, which is prepared for division.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, preparing to use the harmonic scalpel to divide the last supply vessel to the colon, having already taken all pedicles.
The surgeon navigates between the visible vasculature to reach the colon, taking the final few bites to complete the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the transverse colon and the descending colon, with the stomach visible below. The area marked for resection includes the arch vessel.

The surgeon clips an arch vessel on the descending colonic side, continuing the dissection of the transverse colon and the remaining flimsy part of the splenic flexure. This creates a clear demarcation for specimen retrieval, aiding in the decision for a healthy colocolic anastomosis.
The surgeon has completed the division of the Drummond's arch vessel and the lateral mobilization of the colon, with visible separation of tissue planes.

The surgeon focuses on the splenic part of the colonic attachment, applying pressure to manipulate the tissue. The area of growth is identified, and a fifteen-centimeter length of colon is visually assessed.
The surgeon indicates the demarcation point on the descending colon side, similar to the transverse colon side, as the mobilization of the colon is nearly complete.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum and omentum from the colon, preserving the arch vessel while clipping another arch vessel. The colon is mobilized downward.
The surgeon identifies the colon, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the laxity of the colon in the area where it has been mobilized, showing the freed section of the colon with visible separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the omentum from the colon, visibly demarcating the colon as part of the specimen. The stomach is lifted to enhance visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the colon downwards while the assistant pulls the omentum upwards, completing the mobilization of the colonic flexure near the tail of the pancreas. The surgical field reveals the pancreas and surrounding structures, with attention to any potential bleeding.

The surgeon begins to demarcate the area after clipping the arch vessel, revealing a healthy sigmoid colon. The team proceeds to mobilize the sigmoid colon by dividing the peripheral adhesions, preparing for an end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing the preserved epiploic vessels. The anatomy is clearly visible due to the patient's thinness, ensuring differentiation from colonic structures.
The surgeon performs a radical left hemicolectomy, revealing the inferior part of the pancreas. Lymph node clearance at the root is visible, and the surgeon divides the last bead of tissue.

The surgeon completes the dissection and prepares to convert to a small upper midline incision for specimen removal. The transverse and descending colon have been mobilized, allowing for a colocolic open anastomosis through the same incision. Radical lymphatic clearance has been performed.

The surgeon identifies the inferior border of the pancreas and the duodenojejunal flexure, with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and renal vein visible. The pancreas is fully exposed, showing its entire length. The spleen is partially visible, and there is noticeable puckering around the growth. The colon is clearly demarcated in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips the arch vessel supplying the area of demarcation on the descending colon. The growth is visible with a fifteen-centimeter margin, and the sigmoid colon is mobilized.

The surgeon discusses the possibility of performing an intracorporeal anastomosis between the colons, mentioning the use of a staple technique. The surgical field shows both colons prepared for anastomosis. A gauze piece is noted inside the surgical area, and the instrument count is confirmed to be correct.

The surgical team confirms the liver is intact with no bleeding or bile leak. They prepare to convert the procedure to an open approach to remove the specimen, discussing whether to use a bag for extraction.
The surgeon uses a wound protection device to facilitate the removal of the left lobe of the liver along with metastatic tissue, preventing contamination of the surgical wound.
The surgeon places seromuscular sutures using 4-0 PDS and silk on the mesenteric side, preparing for a single-layer closure with continuous running sutures.
The surgeon begins suturing with PDS 4-0, creating loose curves in the suture line, and instructs to cut the suture.
The surgeon places a surgical drain near the site of the anastomosis, preparing to show the completed anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a single-layer continuous anastomosis using PDS sutures, with additional seromuscular stitches applied for reinforcement, resulting in a lax anastomosis.

The surgeon assesses the vascularity at both ends of the bowel, confirming good blood supply. The mesentery is oriented correctly and positioned behind the bowel. No lavage or wash is performed, and a drain is placed.
